edges.description: Striatal degeneration produces the hyperkinetic motor phenotype of chorea.
Progressive cortical and frontostriatal degeneration drives cognitive decline severe enough to manifest as dementia.
Basal-ganglia network degeneration can produce hypokinetic parkinsonian features.
Cortical and frontostriatal involvement contributes to mood and behavioral manifestations.

nodes.description: Chorea is the most consistently reported hyperkinetic motor manifestation of HDL2.

evidence.reference_title: A Systematic Review of the Huntington Disease-Like 2 Phenotype.

nodes.description: Progressive cognitive decline frequently evolves to dementia in HDL2.

evidence.reference_title: A Systematic Review of the Huntington Disease-Like 2 Phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Dementia was reported in 74% patients, and Parkinsonism in 37%.

nodes.description: HDL2 causes prominent degeneration of the striatum and cerebral cortex, and MRI volumetry shows widespread intracerebral atrophy with relatively smaller thalamic volumes than in matched HD cohorts.

evidence.reference_title: Huntington's disease like-2: review and update.
evidence.snippet: Like HD, the neuropathology of HDL2 features prominent cortical and striatal atrophy and intranuclear inclusions.

evidence.reference_title: Emerging differences between Huntington's disease-like 2 and Huntington's disease: A comparison using MRI brain volumetry.
evidence.snippet: Overall, intracerebral volumes were smaller in both affected groups compared to the control group. Comparing the HDL2 and HD groups across multiple covariates, cortical and subcortical volumes were similar with the exception that the HDL2 thalamic volumes were smaller.

nodes.description: HDL2 is initiated by a pathogenic CTG/CAG repeat expansion localized to a variably spliced exon of JPH3, the gene encoding junctophilin-3.

evidence.reference_title: A repeat expansion in the gene encoding junctophilin-3 is associated with Huntington disease-like 2.
evidence.snippet: We now report the cloning of this expansion and its localization to a variably spliced exon of JPH3 (encoding junctophilin-3), a gene involved in the formation of junctional membrane structures.

evidence.reference_title: Huntington disease-like 2: insight into neurodegeneration from an African disease.
evidence.snippet: Huntington disease (HD)-like 2 (HDL2) is a rare genetic disease caused by an expanded trinucleotide repeat in the JPH3 gene (encoding junctophilin 3) that shows remarkable clinical similarity to HD.

nodes.description: Hypokinetic features including rigidity and bradykinesia are common in HDL2 and can contribute substantially to disability.

evidence.reference_title: A Systematic Review of the Huntington Disease-Like 2 Phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Dementia was reported in 74% patients, and Parkinsonism in 37%.

nodes.description: Psychiatric disease is a core part of the HDL2 phenotype and commonly includes mood disturbance and behavioral change.

evidence.reference_title: A Systematic Review of the Huntington Disease-Like 2 Phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Psychiatric features were reported in 44 out of 47 cases.

evidence.reference_title: The Neuropsychiatry of Huntington Disease-Like 2: A Comparison with Huntington's Disease.
evidence.snippet: HDL2 patients presented with psychiatric symptoms involving mood disturbances and behavioural changes that were not significantly different from those in the HD group.

nodes.description: HDL2 brain shows reduced JPH3 transcript and full-length protein expression, consistent with partial loss of junctophilin-3 function and impaired neuronal calcium-handling biology.

biological_processes: regulation of cytosolic calcium ion concentration

evidence.reference_title: Loss of junctophilin-3 contributes to Huntington disease-like 2 pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: However, JPH3 transcripts and full-length JPH3 protein are decreased in HDL2 brain, and Jph3 hemizygous and null mice exhibit abnormal motor function.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of junctophilin-3 contributes to Huntington disease-like 2 pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Jph3 hemizygous and null mice exhibit abnormal motor function.

nodes.description: Expanded JPH3 repeat-containing transcripts are implicated in HDL2 through a toxic RNA gain-of-function mechanism involving CUG-repeat RNA.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of junctophilin-3 contributes to Huntington disease-like 2 pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: We have previously demonstrated the potential pathogenic properties of JPH3 transcripts containing expanded CUG repeats.

evidence.reference_title: Huntington disease-like 2: insight into neurodegeneration from an African disease.
evidence.snippet: The pathogenesis of HDL2 remains unclear but it is proposed to occur through several mechanisms, including loss of protein function and RNA and/or protein toxicity.
